label: delphi-r1-p7
system:
You are performing one specific cognitive operation as an isolated component of a legal-exploration pipeline. You never see the other components' outputs, and nobody sees yours until a separate neutral aggregation step. Ground rules for every component:
- The scenario is an invented hypothetical; there is no real client. Your output goes to a licensed California attorney who will do the actual legal reasoning. You generate angles to pursue and things to verify — not advice to be relied on.
- Cite specific authorities (statutes, cases, regulations) when they genuinely support a point. Every citation is automatically flagged UNVERIFIED for human checking, so cite what you believe is correct, but never fabricate quotations or pinpoint holdings you are unsure of. When unsure, write "verify whether X holds Y" instead of asserting it.
- Perform ONLY the operation described below. Do not produce a general-purpose legal memo.

Operation: you are ONE independent panelist in a Delphi elicitation. There is no
discussion; you answer alone. Give your honest probability, not a diplomatic one.
Do not hedge toward 50 to be safe — commit to your analysis.
prompt:
## Facts (invented hypothetical — no real parties)

The Sierra Vista Municipal Water District (invented) serves ~140,000 people in a chronically drought-stressed service area. After a rate study and a majority-protest hearing that drew protests from ~9% of parcels, the board adopted a "Water Resilience Surcharge": $0.85 per hundred cubic feet on residential usage above Tier 2 (the surcharge falls on roughly the top 22% of residential users), projected to raise $4.1M/year into a restricted fund spent as follows: 55% on turf-replacement and high-efficiency-appliance rebates that prioritize low-income customers; 30% on debt service for a recycled-water plant; 15% on "water ambassador" conservation-education staff.

The rate study concludes that high-tier consumption imposes marginal supply costs roughly 1.6× base-tier costs (peaking capacity, drought-reserve purchases), but it derives the $0.85 figure in part from what it calls "conservation-incentive pricing" rather than strictly from cost allocation, and the district concedes the rebate program mostly benefits customers other than those paying the surcharge. A ratepayer association has sued, alleging the surcharge exceeds the proportional cost of service attributable to the payers' parcels in violation of article XIII D, section 6(b) of the California Constitution, citing Capistrano Taxpayers Association v. City of San Juan Capistrano. The district defends the structure under its conservation mandate (Cal. Const. art. X, § 2; Water Code § 375) and argues that, in the alternative, the surcharge is not a property-related fee at all.

## The question

Does the Water Resilience Surcharge violate article XIII D, section 6(b)'s cost-of-service and proportionality requirements? How does Capistrano apply to a tier surcharge that is partly cost-justified (1.6×) and partly incentive-priced, with proceeds partially redistributed as rebates to non-payers? Can article X, section 2's conservation mandate be reconciled with 218, or does 218 win? Is there a colorable argument the surcharge is a tax requiring voter approval (Prop 26), or a non-property-related charge? What is the litigation exposure (refunds, fees) and how could the structure be repaired?

## Proposition to assess

"A court would invalidate the Water Resilience Surcharge, in whole or in substantial part, as violating the cost-of-service requirements of California Constitution article XIII D, section 6(b)."

Estimate the probability (0–100) that this proposition is correct, with your reasoning. rationales = your independent load-bearing reasons, one sentence each. authorities = specific authorities you are actually relying on (they will be flagged for human verification). biggest_uncertainty = the single thing you are least sure of.

Analytical starting point for you: Begin from the advice a cautious public-agency counsel would give, then ask whether caution is legally required or merely prudent.
text: {"probability":70,"confidence":"medium","position_summary":"More likely than not a court invalidates at least a substantial part of the surcharge — most plausibly the increment attributable to \"conservation-incentive pricing\" above the documented 1.6x cost differential, and/or the revenue-use structure insofar as roughly 55% of proceeds fund rebates flowing predominantly to non-payers — because the stated facts contain the district's own admissions that map closely onto what Capistrano condemned. A cautious public-agency lawyer would flag this rate as high litigation risk pre-adoption; nothing in article X, section 2 or Water Code section 375 legally excuses the failure to tie the incremental rate and the rebate cross-subsidy to cost of service, so the caution here tracks genuine legal exposure rather than mere risk-aversion. The district's fallback \"not a property-related fee\" argument is very likely foreclosed by Bighorn-Desert View Water Agency v. Vejil, and the Proposition 26 \"voter-approval tax\" theory is a viable but secondary plaintiff argument if the 218 cost-of-service defense fails outright.","rationales":["Capistrano Taxpayers Assn. v. City of San Juan Capistrano (2015) 235 Cal.App.4th 1493 held tiered water rates must correspond to the actual marginal cost of service at that tier and rejected conservation-based incentive pricing as a substitute for cost allocation, and the district's own concession that $0.85 was derived 'in part' from conservation-incentive pricing rather than strict cost allocation is close to a facial admission of that exact defect for the non-cost-justified increment.","The district's concession that the rebate program (55% of restricted-fund revenue) 'mostly benefits customers other than those paying the surcharge' independently implicates article XIII D, section 6(b)(2)-(3) — fee revenue may not exceed, and must correlate to, the proportional cost of service attributable to the fee-paying parcels — and this cross-subsidization defect is analytically distinct from, and arguably more damaging than, the tier-pricing methodology issue.","Article X, section 2's conservation mandate and Water Code section 375 are general policy provisions that California courts have treated as unable to override the specific, later-enacted, self-executing cost-of-service mandate of article XIII D, section 6(b) (this is essentially the holding rejected-argument posture in Capistrano itself), so the district's 'conservation mandate reconciliation' defense is doctrinally weak standing alone.","Bighorn-Desert View Water Agency v. Vejil (2006) 39 Cal.4th 205 held that volumetric water charges for ongoing service are property-related fees subject to article XIII D, which makes the district's alternative 'not a property-related fee at all' argument a low-probability escape hatch.","The 1.6x documented marginal-cost differential (peaking capacity, drought-reserve purchases) gives the district a genuine, non-trivial cost-of-service anchor, so a court could plausibly sever and uphold a cost-justified portion of the surcharge up to roughly that ratio while striking only the incremental 'incentive' overlay and/or the rebate-funded 55% tranche, meaning full facial invalidation of the entire surcharge is less likely than a substantial partial invalidation."],"authorities":["Cal. Const. art. XIII D, § 6(b)(1)-(5)","Capistrano Taxpayers Assn. v. City of San Juan Capistrano (2015) 235 Cal.App.4th 1493","Bighorn-Desert View Water Agency v. Vejil (2006) 39 Cal.4th 205","Cal. Const. art. X, § 2","Water Code § 375","Cal. Const. art. XIII C, § 1(e) (Prop 26 'tax' definition and fee exception)","Griffith v. Pajaro Valley Water Mgmt. Agency (2013) 220 Cal.App.4th 586 (cited for comparison on fee-for-service correlation; verify pinpoint applicability)","Coziahr v. Otay Water Dist. (2024) — post-Capistrano tiered-rate case; verify holding and whether it narrows or reinforces Capistrano before relying on it"],"biggest_uncertainty":"Whether a reviewing court would treat the \"conservation-incentive pricing\" and rebate cross-subsidy defects as severable from the genuinely cost-justified 1.6x portion of the rate (yielding a narrow, partial remedy/refund) versus treating the whole tiered surcharge as an indivisible, tainted rate structure requiring wholesale invalidation — this severability question, plus uncertainty about how recent post-Capistrano appellate authority (e.g., Coziahr v. Otay Water Dist., which I cannot verify with confidence) has refined the standard for conservation-linked tiered pricing, is the single largest driver of variance in my estimate."}

data.position_summary: More likely than not a court invalidates at least a substantial part of the surcharge — most plausibly the increment attributable to "conservation-incentive pricing" above the documented 1.6x cost differential, and/or the revenue-use structure insofar as roughly 55% of proceeds fund rebates flowing predominantly to non-payers — because the stated facts contain the district's own admissions that map closely onto what Capistrano condemned. A cautious public-agency lawyer would flag this rate as high litigation risk pre-adoption; nothing in article X, section 2 or Water Code section 375 legally excuses the failure to tie the incremental rate and the rebate cross-subsidy to cost of service, so the caution here tracks genuine legal exposure rather than mere risk-aversion. The district's fallback "not a property-related fee" argument is very likely foreclosed by Bighorn-Desert View Water Agency v. Vejil, and the Proposition 26 "voter-approval tax" theory is a viable but secondary plaintiff argument if the 218 cost-of-service defense fails outright.
rationales: Capistrano Taxpayers Assn. v. City of San Juan Capistrano (2015) 235 Cal.App.4th 1493 held tiered water rates must correspond to the actual marginal cost of service at that tier and rejected conservation-based incentive pricing as a substitute for cost allocation, and the district's own concession that $0.85 was derived 'in part' from conservation-incentive pricing rather than strict cost allocation is close to a facial admission of that exact defect for the non-cost-justified increment.

The district's concession that the rebate program (55% of restricted-fund revenue) 'mostly benefits customers other than those paying the surcharge' independently implicates article XIII D, section 6(b)(2)-(3) — fee revenue may not exceed, and must correlate to, the proportional cost of service attributable to the fee-paying parcels — and this cross-subsidization defect is analytically distinct from, and arguably more damaging than, the tier-pricing methodology issue.

Article X, section 2's conservation mandate and Water Code section 375 are general policy provisions that California courts have treated as unable to override the specific, later-enacted, self-executing cost-of-service mandate of article XIII D, section 6(b) (this is essentially the holding rejected-argument posture in Capistrano itself), so the district's 'conservation mandate reconciliation' defense is doctrinally weak standing alone.

Bighorn-Desert View Water Agency v. Vejil (2006) 39 Cal.4th 205 held that volumetric water charges for ongoing service are property-related fees subject to article XIII D, which makes the district's alternative 'not a property-related fee at all' argument a low-probability escape hatch.

The 1.6x documented marginal-cost differential (peaking capacity, drought-reserve purchases) gives the district a genuine, non-trivial cost-of-service anchor, so a court could plausibly sever and uphold a cost-justified portion of the surcharge up to roughly that ratio while striking only the incremental 'incentive' overlay and/or the rebate-funded 55% tranche, meaning full facial invalidation of the entire surcharge is less likely than a substantial partial invalidation.
authorities: Capistrano Taxpayers Assn. v. City of San Juan Capistrano (2015) 235 Cal.App.4th 1493
Bighorn-Desert View Water Agency v. Vejil (2006) 39 Cal.4th 205
Cal. Const. art. XIII C, § 1(e) (Prop 26 'tax' definition and fee exception)
Griffith v. Pajaro Valley Water Mgmt. Agency (2013) 220 Cal.App.4th 586 (cited for comparison on fee-for-service correlation; verify pinpoint applicability)
Coziahr v. Otay Water Dist. (2024) — post-Capistrano tiered-rate case; verify holding and whether it narrows or reinforces Capistrano before relying on it
data.biggest_uncertainty: Whether a reviewing court would treat the "conservation-incentive pricing" and rebate cross-subsidy defects as severable from the genuinely cost-justified 1.6x portion of the rate (yielding a narrow, partial remedy/refund) versus treating the whole tiered surcharge as an indivisible, tainted rate structure requiring wholesale invalidation — this severability question, plus uncertainty about how recent post-Capistrano appellate authority (e.g., Coziahr v. Otay Water Dist., which I cannot verify with confidence) has refined the standard for conservation-linked tiered pricing, is the single largest driver of variance in my estimate.
